text: So I'll be presenting robotic NICE procedure with transrectal ileocecectomy for deep infiltrative endometriosis. I have no disclosures. The incidence of bowel involvement in endometriosis is three to thirty seven percent, with ninety percent of that being in the rectum or sigmoid. Indications for surgery include chronic pain, infertility, obstruction. Challenges often include obliterated anatomy and planes. I'm presenting a case demonstrating surgical management of deep infiltrative endometriosis with multifocal involvement involving the rectosigmoid terminal ileum and cecum resulting in chronic pain and recurrent bowel obstruction.

Upon entry into the pelvis, the surgeon begins by taking down the lateral attachments to the sigmoid colon. The lateral abdominal wall and fallopian tube are visibly drawn into an endometrioma, which is being released. The dissection continues distally, revealing the uterus and sigmoid colon, highlighting the obliteration of anatomical planes. The surgeon encounters bleeding while freeing the rectum and reaches the rectovaginal plane, characterized by yellow fat. The dissection proceeds laterally, identifying and protecting the left ureter. The right perirectal plane is taken down, freeing the rectum. Proximal and distal transection points are identified, and the bowel is transected using a vessel sealer, preparing for transrectal extraction.

The surgeon addresses the ileocecal disease area, where endometriosis is visible. The mesentery has been dissected, and a 60mm linear stapler with a blue load is inserted to transect the cecal portion and the terminal ileum.

The surgeon performs a transrectal extraction of the bowel segment. On the anti-mesenteric side, aneurotomies are made on the proximal and distal limbs. A stapler is inserted to perform a side-to-side anastomosis. The common aneurotomy is closed using a linear stapler with two staple loads to prevent stenosis, and the stapler is removed through the rectum.

The surgeon inserts a circular stapler and releases the anvil, placing a purse string suture around the sigmoid colon using a 3-0 V-Loc suture. The anvil is secured with a Vicryl endo loop. The procedure is repeated at the distal portion, deploying the spike and securing it with another Vicryl endo loop.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis following the resection, checking tissue viability with indocyanine green (ICG) and conducting an air insufflation test. The procedure utilizes the rectum as a natural orifice for transrectal extraction.
